title: Longest Palindromic Substring
description: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.

examples.explanation: Both 'bab' and 'aba' are valid answers of length 3.
The longest palindromic substring is 'bb' with length 2.
No two-character palindrome. Either single character is valid.
The entire string is a palindrome.

constraints: s consists of only digits and English letters
intuition: Every palindrome has a center. For odd-length palindromes the center is a single character. For even-length palindromes the center is between two equal characters. The expand-around-center approach exploits this: for each possible center (n centers for odd, n-1 centers for even, total 2n-1), expand outward as long as characters on both sides match. The longest expansion from any center is the answer. This is O(n^2) time but O(1) space — no extra array needed. The key insight is that you never need to 'check' whether a substring is a palindrome from scratch — you grow it character by character, stopping the moment it fails.

palindrome_types.odd_length: Center is a single character. Call helper(i, i, s). Expands from one character outward symmetrically.
palindrome_types.even_length: Center is between two characters. Call helper(i, i+1, s). Starts by checking if s[i]==s[i+1], then expands outward.

helper_explanation.how_it_works: Starts with a center defined by (left, right). While both pointers are in bounds AND s[left]==s[right], expand: left--, right++. When the loop exits (either out of bounds or mismatch), the palindrome is s[left+1 : right]. The +1 and no +1 on right are because after the last valid expansion, left was decremented one too far and right was incremented one too far.
helper_explanation.return_slice: s[left+1 : right] — left+1 corrects the over-decrement, right is already exclusive in Python slicing so no adjustment needed.

why_two_calls_per_center: For each index i, we make two calls: helper(i,i,s) for odd-length palindromes centered at i, and helper(i,i+1,s) for even-length palindromes centered between i and i+1. Without both calls, even-length palindromes like 'abba' would be missed entirely (no single character center).
return_slice_derivation: After the while loop exits, left and right point one step BEYOND the last valid palindrome boundary. The last valid palindrome was s[left+1 .. right-1]. In Python, s[left+1 : right] correctly captures this because Python slice end is exclusive — s[a:b] = s[a], s[a+1], ..., s[b-1].

approach_1_expand_center.name: Expand Around Center (your solution)
approach_1_expand_center.description: For each of the 2n-1 possible centers, expand outward. Track the longest palindrome found. Two cases per index: odd-length (single center) and even-length (pair center).

approach_2_brute_force.name: Brute Force
approach_2_brute_force.description: Check every possible substring (O(n^2) pairs) and verify each is a palindrome (O(n)). Three nested loops total. Correct but too slow for n=1000.

approach_3_dp.name: Dynamic Programming
approach_3_dp.description: Build a 2D table dp[i][j] = True if s[i..j] is a palindrome. Base cases: all single chars (dp[i][i]=True), pairs (dp[i][i+1] = s[i]==s[i+1]). Transition: dp[i][j] = (s[i]==s[j]) and dp[i+1][j-1]. Fill by increasing substring length.
approach_3_dp.recurrence: dp[i][j] = True if s[i] == s[j] AND dp[i+1][j-1] == True (for length >= 3)

approach_4_manachers.name: Manacher's Algorithm
approach_4_manachers.description: The optimal algorithm. Transforms the string by inserting '#' between characters (handling odd/even uniformly). Maintains a right boundary and center of the currently furthest-reaching palindrome. Uses previously computed palindrome radii to skip redundant work. Returns the center and radius of the longest palindrome.
approach_4_manachers.key_insight: If we are inside a known palindrome, our mirror position has already been computed. We can initialize our radius from the mirror instead of starting from 0 — avoiding redundant comparisons.
approach_4_manachers.code:
def longestPalindrome(s):
    # transform: 'abc' -> '#a#b#c#'
    t = '#' + '#'.join(s) + '#'
    n = len(t)
    p = [0] * n  # p[i] = palindrome radius at i in transformed string
    center = right = 0
    best_center = best_radius = 0

    for i in range(n):
        # mirror of i with respect to current center
        mirror = 2 * center - i

        if i < right:
            # initialize from mirror, but don't exceed right boundary
            p[i] = min(right - i, p[mirror])

        # try to expand beyond known boundary
        while (i + p[i] + 1 < n and
               i - p[i] - 1 >= 0 and
               t[i + p[i] + 1] == t[i - p[i] - 1]):
            p[i] += 1

        # update center and right boundary
        if i + p[i] > right:
            center = i
            right = i + p[i]

        # track best palindrome
        if p[i] > best_radius:
            best_radius = p[i]
            best_center = i

    # extract from transformed string back to original
    start = (best_center - best_radius) // 2
    return s[start : start + best_radius]
    # Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

rows: Easy to understand, too slow for n=1000
Classic DP, clear recurrence, high memory
Best practical choice, simple code, optimal space
Theoretically optimal, complex to implement, rarely needed in interviews

dp_recurrence_explained.base_single: dp[i][i] = True for all i — every single character is trivially a palindrome.
dp_recurrence_explained.base_pair: dp[i][i+1] = (s[i] == s[i+1]) — a two-character string is a palindrome iff both characters are equal.
dp_recurrence_explained.transition: dp[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) AND dp[i+1][j-1] — outer characters must match AND the inner substring must already be a palindrome.
dp_recurrence_explained.why_fill_by_length: dp[i][j] depends on dp[i+1][j-1] which is a strictly smaller substring. Filling by increasing length ensures the inner result is always computed before the outer.

manachers_transform_explained.why_insert_hash: Inserting '#' between every character converts all palindromes to odd-length in the transformed string. 'abba' (even, length 4) becomes '#a#b#b#a#' (odd, length 9). This lets us use a single formula for both odd and even palindromes.
manachers_transform_explained.radius_meaning: p[i] is the radius of the palindrome centered at i in the transformed string. The actual palindrome length in the original string is p[i] (the '#' characters don't contribute to original length).
manachers_transform_explained.extract_back: If best center in transformed string is best_center with radius best_radius: start in original = (best_center - best_radius) // 2. Length = best_radius.

steps: i=0 'b': c1=helper(0,0)='b'(len1), c2=helper(0,1): s[0]='b'!=s[1]='a' -> returns ''(len0). candidate='b'. result='b'.

i=1 'a': c1=helper(1,1): expand left=0,right=2 s[0]='b'==s[2]='b'->left=-1,right=3. s[-1] OOB. return s[0:3]='bab'(len3). c2=helper(1,2): s[1]='a'!=s[2]='b'->returns ''(len0). candidate='bab'. result='bab'.

i=2 'b': c1=helper(2,2): expand left=1,right=3 s[1]='a'==s[3]='a'->left=0,right=4. s[0]='b'==s[4]='d'? No. return s[1:4]='aba'(len3). c2=helper(2,3): s[2]='b'!=s[3]='a'->returns ''(len0). candidate='aba'(len3). len(3)>=len('bab'=3) so result='aba'.

i=3 'd': c1=helper(3,3): expand left=2,right=4 s[2]='b'!=s[4]='d'->return s[3:4]='d'(len1). c2=helper(3,4): right=4 out of bounds immediately ->return s[4:4]=''(len0). candidate='d'. result stays 'aba'.
i=4 'd': c1=helper(4,4): left=3 s[3]='a'!=s[5] OOB -> return s[4:5]='d'(len1). c2=helper(4,5): right=5 OOB -> return ''(len0). candidate='d'. result stays 'aba'.

python_gotchas: The helper returns s[left+1 : right] — not s[left : right] and not s[left+1 : right+1]. After the loop, left is one position before the palindrome's start and right is one position after its end. left+1 corrects the over-decrement; right is already the exclusive Python slice end.

helper(i, i+1, s) for even palindromes: if s[i] != s[i+1] the while condition fails immediately (s[left] != s[right]) and the function returns s[i+1:i+1] = '' (empty string). This correctly contributes length 0 for non-matching pairs.

The candidate comparison uses >= not > for 'if len(candidate) >= len(result)'. Using > would skip same-length palindromes found later — still correct but may return a different valid answer. >= ensures the last equally long palindrome is chosen.
Python string slicing never throws IndexError — s[left+1:right] where left+1 > right returns ''. This is how helper handles the case where no palindrome expansion happens.

For the DP approach, the inner loop must go over increasing length FIRST, then starting index. If you loop over i and j naively without respecting length order, dp[i+1][j-1] may not be computed yet when dp[i][j] is being filled.
s[::-1] is O(n) and creates a new string — checking sub == sub[::-1] in the brute force is O(n) per check, making the full brute force O(n^3) not O(n^2).

related_problems.title: Palindromic Substrings (LC 647)
related_problems.relation: Count all palindromic substrings instead of finding the longest one. Uses the exact same expand-around-center approach — instead of tracking the longest, increment a counter for each successful expansion step.

related_problems.title: Palindrome Partitioning (LC 131)
related_problems.relation: Partition the string into all substrings that are palindromes. Uses DP palindrome checking (same dp[i][j] table) as preprocessing, then DFS/backtracking to enumerate partitions.

related_problems.title: Shortest Palindrome (LC 214)
related_problems.relation: Find the shortest palindrome by adding characters to the front. Requires finding the longest palindromic prefix — solved with KMP or Manacher's.

related_problems.title: Valid Palindrome (LC 125)
related_problems.relation: Check if a string is a palindrome after filtering non-alphanumeric characters. Uses two-pointer converging from both ends — the reverse of expand-around-center.

related_problems.title: Palindrome Pairs (LC 336)
related_problems.relation: Given a list of words, find all pairs that form a palindrome when concatenated. Much harder — uses trie or hash map with palindrome suffix/prefix checks.

mcqs.question: Why does the helper function return s[left+1 : right] instead of s[left : right+1]?
options: Python slicing is exclusive on the left side

After the while loop exits, left has been decremented one step past the valid start and right has been incremented one step past the valid end. The true palindrome is s[left+1 .. right-1]. In Python s[left+1 : right] correctly captures this because slice end is exclusive.
left+1 skips the center character which should not be included
The +1 handles the even-length palindrome case specifically
mcqs.explanation: The while loop's last successful expansion decrements left and increments right. Then the failing condition is checked — but left and right are already at their over-extended values. So the valid palindrome boundaries are left+1 (correct the over-decrement) and right-1 (correct the over-increment). Python slice s[a:b] gives s[a..b-1], so s[left+1 : right] = s[left+1 .. right-1]. Correct.

mcqs.question: Why are two helper calls made per index (helper(i,i,s) and helper(i,i+1,s))?
options: One call handles left expansion, the other handles right expansion

helper(i,i,s) finds odd-length palindromes centered at a single character. helper(i,i+1,s) finds even-length palindromes centered between two characters. Without both, even-length palindromes like 'abba' or 'bb' would be completely missed.
The second call is a backup in case the first call returns empty
One call checks forward, the other checks backward
mcqs.explanation: Odd palindromes (aba, racecar) have a single character center — start both pointers at i. Even palindromes (abba, bb) have their center between two characters — start left at i and right at i+1. If s[i] == s[i+1], expansion begins. If not, the function returns '' immediately. Both types are covered by these two calls at every index.

mcqs.question: What does helper(i, i+1, s) return when s[i] != s[i+1]?
options: s[i] alone — falls back to single character
None — the function has no return for failed cases
'' (empty string) — the while condition s[left]==s[right] fails immediately, loop body never runs, returns s[i+1:i+1] which is ''
Raises an IndexError since i+1 might be out of bounds
mcqs.explanation: The while loop checks s[left] == s[right] first. If s[i] != s[i+1], the loop body never executes. left remains i and right remains i+1. Return s[left+1 : right] = s[i+1 : i+1] = '' (empty string). Python slices where start==end return ''. This correctly contributes length 0 to the candidate comparison.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the expand-around-center approach and why?
options: O(n) — each character is visited once
O(n^2) — for each of the O(n) centers, expansion takes O(n) time in the worst case. Total: O(n) * O(n) = O(n^2).
O(n^3) — three nested loops in the helper
O(n log n) — binary search is used to find palindrome boundaries
mcqs.explanation: There are 2n-1 possible centers (n single-character + n-1 between-character). For each center, expansion takes O(n) worst case (full string is a palindrome). Total: O(n) centers * O(n) expansion = O(n^2). In practice much faster — most centers expand only a few steps.

mcqs.question: What is the DP recurrence for dp[i][j] and what do the base cases handle?
options: dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] OR dp[i][j-1] — take either half

dp[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) AND dp[i+1][j-1]. Base cases: dp[i][i] = True (single chars) and dp[i][i+1] = (s[i]==s[i+1]) (pairs). dp[i+1][j-1] for a length-2 substring would be dp[i+1][i] which is a length-0 or negative range — treated as True.
dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] AND dp[i][j+1]
dp[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) only — inner check is implicit
mcqs.explanation: A substring s[i..j] is a palindrome if its outer characters match (s[i]==s[j]) AND the inner substring s[i+1..j-1] is also a palindrome (dp[i+1][j-1]). For length 1 (i==j): trivially true. For length 2 (j==i+1): true iff s[i]==s[j]. For length >= 3: the recurrence applies. Note: for j = i+2 (length 3), dp[i+1][i+1] is the base case — a single character, always True.

mcqs.question: Why must the DP table be filled by increasing substring LENGTH rather than iterating i and j directly?
options: Longer substrings are more likely to be palindromes

dp[i][j] depends on dp[i+1][j-1] — a strictly shorter substring. If you iterate by i and then j without respecting length order, dp[i+1][j-1] might not be computed yet when you compute dp[i][j], giving wrong results.
Iterating by length is faster than iterating by index pairs
Python 2D arrays must be filled in row-major order
mcqs.explanation: Topological dependency: dp[i][j] requires dp[i+1][j-1] which has length j-i-1 = (j-i+1)-2. So a palindrome of length L depends on a palindrome of length L-2. Fill order must ensure smaller lengths are computed first. Iterating 'for length in range(1, n+1)' guarantees this. Iterating by i then j (row-major) would compute dp[0][3] before dp[1][2] — incorrect.

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity of the DP approach and can it be optimized?
options: O(n) — only one row of the table is needed at a time

O(n^2) as written — the full n x n table is stored. It can be partially optimized but not easily to O(1) because dp[i][j] needs dp[i+1][j-1] which is a diagonal dependency, making row-by-row elimination tricky.
O(1) — the dp table is computed in place on the string
O(n^2) and cannot be reduced at all
mcqs.explanation: The full dp table is O(n^2). Unlike some DP problems where you only need the previous row, here dp[i][j] depends on dp[i+1][j-1] which is a diagonal element. This makes simple 1D rolling optimization non-trivial. Some implementations use two 1D arrays but the code complexity increases significantly. For n=1000, O(n^2) = 10^6 booleans — manageable. The expand-around-center approach avoids this entirely.

mcqs.question: What transformation does Manacher's algorithm apply and why?
options: Reverses the string to detect palindromes from both ends

Inserts a '#' separator between every character (and at the ends): 'abc' -> '#a#b#c#'. This converts all palindromes to odd-length in the transformed string, allowing a single unified formula for both odd and even original palindromes.
Converts all characters to lowercase for case-insensitive comparison
Removes duplicate characters to speed up palindrome detection
mcqs.explanation: 'abba' (even length 4) becomes '#a#b#b#a#' (odd length 9, center '#' between two b's). 'aba' (odd length 3) becomes '#a#b#a#' (odd length 7, center 'b'). The '#' characters never match each other for actual character comparisons but ensure all palindromes are odd in the transformed string. This eliminates the odd/even case split that requires two helper calls in expand-around-center.

mcqs.question: What is the key optimization in Manacher's algorithm that achieves O(n)?
options: It uses binary search to find palindrome boundaries

It maintains the rightmost palindrome boundary seen so far. For any center i inside that boundary, its mirror position has already been computed. p[i] can be initialized from p[mirror] instead of 0 — skipping redundant character comparisons. Each character is 'compared' at most twice total across all expansions.
It uses divide and conquer to split the string in half
It precomputes a hash of every substring for O(1) palindrome checks
mcqs.explanation: The key: if center i is within the current rightmost palindrome [center-right, right], then i's mirror (2*center - i) has already been fully computed. p[i] = min(right - i, p[mirror]) initializes p[i] without re-examining those characters. Expansion then tries to go beyond the known boundary. Total expansions across all centers = O(n) (each position enters the expansion loop at most twice). This amortized argument gives O(n).

mcqs.question: For s = "cbbd", trace helper(1, 2, s) (the even-length call at i=1).
options: Returns 'cbbd' — the full string
Returns 'bb' — s[1]='b'==s[2]='b', expand: left=0,right=3. s[0]='c'!=s[3]='d', stop. Return s[1:3]='bb'.
Returns '' — 'b'!='b' is False so loop never runs
Returns 'b' — single character only
mcqs.explanation: helper(1, 2, s): left=1, right=2. s[1]='b'==s[2]='b' -> left=0, right=3. s[0]='c' vs s[3]='d': not equal -> break. Return s[left+1:right] = s[1:3] = 'bb'. Length 2. This is the correct answer for 'cbbd'.

mcqs.question: For s = "racecar", which index i and which call (odd/even) finds the full palindrome?
options: i=3, even-length call helper(3,4,s)
i=3, odd-length call helper(3,3,s) — center character 'e' at index 3, expansion reaches the full string
i=0, even-length call stretches to the end
i=6, odd-length call going backwards
mcqs.explanation: s = 'racecar', center is 'e' at index 3. helper(3,3,s): left=3,right=3. s[3]='e'==s[3]='e' -> left=2,right=4. s[2]='c'==s[4]='c' -> left=1,right=5. s[1]='a'==s[5]='a' -> left=0,right=6. s[0]='r'==s[6]='r' -> left=-1,right=7. Bounds fail. Return s[0:7]='racecar'. Length 7.

mcqs.question: The candidate comparison uses '>=' (if len(candidate) >= len(result)). What changes if you use '>'?
options: The algorithm becomes incorrect

With '>=', same-length candidates found later overwrite earlier ones — the returned palindrome may differ but is still a valid answer. With '>', the first longest palindrome found is kept. Both are correct per problem statement (any valid answer accepted), but the choice affects WHICH valid answer is returned.
'>=' is required to handle the empty string edge case
Using '>' causes an IndexError on single-character strings
mcqs.explanation: For s='babad': 'bab' is found at i=1, 'aba' at i=2. With '>=': result='aba' (later same-length palindrome). With '>': result='bab' (first longest). LeetCode accepts both. The choice is a tie-breaking policy, not a correctness issue.

mcqs.question: How does the brute force approach verify if a substring is a palindrome, and what makes it O(n^3)?
options: It uses a recursive call which adds one more O(n) factor
It checks sub == sub[::-1] — reversing the substring is O(n) and creates a new string. Combined with O(n^2) substrings to check, total is O(n) * O(n^2) = O(n^3).
The brute force is only O(n^2) — two nested loops
Reversing a string in Python is O(1) using built-in optimization
mcqs.explanation: Two nested loops generate O(n^2) substrings. For each, sub == sub[::-1] reverses the substring — O(n) time and O(n) space for the reversed copy. O(n^2) substrings * O(n) per check = O(n^3). The expand-around-center avoids this by never generating substrings explicitly — it just moves pointers.

mcqs.question: What is the relationship between Longest Palindromic Substring (LC 5) and Palindromic Substrings (LC 647)?
options: LC 647 is strictly harder and requires Manacher's algorithm

Both use the same expand-around-center structure. LC 5 tracks the maximum-length palindrome found. LC 647 counts every palindromic expansion — each successful left--,right++ step in the while loop counts one additional palindromic substring. The helper logic is identical; only the aggregation differs.
LC 647 uses DP while LC 5 uses expand-around-center
They are the same problem with different output types
mcqs.explanation: Same structure, different tracking. LC 5: max_len = max(max_len, current_palindrome_length). LC 647: count += 1 for each step of expansion (each step uncovers a new palindromic substring). The helper loop body can directly increment count instead of expanding to find the full substring.

mcqs.question: For s = "aaaa", trace the algorithm. How many palindromes of length 4 are found?
options: 1 — the full string 'aaaa' is found at i=0
0 — only odd-length palindromes are found
The full string 'aaaa' (even-length) is found at i=1 via helper(1,2,s): s[1]='a'==s[2]='a' -> expand to s[0]='a'==s[3]='a' -> expand, OOB. Return s[0:4]='aaaa'. max_len=4.
2 — found at both i=1 and i=2
mcqs.explanation: i=1, c2=helper(1,2): left=1,right=2. s[1]=='a'==s[2] -> left=0,right=3. s[0]=='a'==s[3] -> left=-1,right=4. OOB. Return s[0:4]='aaaa'. Also i=1 c1=helper(1,1): 'aaa' (length 3). And i=2 c2=helper(2,3): also 'aaaa'. The >= in the comparison means the last found 'aaaa' is kept. All are valid.

mcqs.question: In the DP approach, what is dp[2][2] for s = "babad"?
options: False — s[2]='b' is part of a larger palindrome
True — every single character is a palindrome. dp[i][i] = True for all i.
Depends on the surrounding characters
Undefined — the DP only fills upper-triangular cells
mcqs.explanation: dp[i][i] = True for all i is a base case — every single character is trivially a palindrome (it reads the same forward and backward). This is set unconditionally for all indices before checking pairs or longer substrings. dp[2][2] = True regardless of what s[2] is.

mcqs.question: What happens when helper(i, i+1, s) is called with i = len(s)-1 (last index)?
options: An IndexError is raised because i+1 is out of bounds

The while condition checks 'right < len(s)' first. right = i+1 = len(s), which is NOT < len(s). The loop never runs. Return s[i+1:i+1] = '' (empty). Safe — Python slice indices can be out of bounds without error.
It returns s[len(s)-1] — the last character
It wraps around and compares the last character with the first
mcqs.explanation: The while condition is 'left >= 0 AND right < len(s)'. At i = n-1, right = n. right < n is False immediately. The loop body is skipped. Return s[n:n] = ''. No IndexError — Python's slice indices are clamped gracefully. This is why no boundary check is needed before calling helper(i, i+1, s) even at the last index.

mcqs.question: Which solution approach is best for a coding interview and why?
options: Manacher's algorithm — O(n) is always better
Dynamic Programming — interviewers always want DP solutions

Expand Around Center — O(n^2) time with O(1) space, simple and clean code, easy to explain. Manacher's is O(n) but rarely expected in interviews due to its complexity. DP is O(n^2) time with O(n^2) space — strictly worse than expand-around-center.
Brute Force — shows complete understanding of the problem
mcqs.explanation: Expand-around-center is the sweet spot: O(n^2) time (same as DP), O(1) space (better than DP's O(n^2)), code is short and intuitive, easy to whiteboard and explain. Manacher's is theoretically optimal but complex enough that most interviewers don't expect it and implementing it error-free under pressure is risky. Start with expand-around-center and offer to discuss Manacher's as a follow-up.

mcqs.question: For s = "ac", what does the algorithm return?
options: "ac" — both characters together form a palindrome
"a" — first character returned since no longer palindrome exists
"" — no palindrome found
"c" — last character
mcqs.explanation: i=0: c1=helper(0,0)='a'(len1). c2=helper(0,1): s[0]='a'!=s[1]='c' -> ''(len0). candidate='a'. result='a'. i=1: c1=helper(1,1)='c'(len1). c2=helper(1,2): right=2 OOB -> ''(len0). candidate='c'. len('c')=1 >= len('a')=1 -> result='c'. Return 'c'. Both 'a' and 'c' are valid answers. LeetCode accepts either.

mcqs.question: The DP approach fills the table for length 3+ in a specific order. For s = "aba", trace dp[0][2].
options: dp[0][2] = False because s[0]='a' != s[2]='a' is false — they do match
dp[0][2] = True. s[0]='a'==s[2]='a' AND dp[1][1]=True (base case, single 'b'). So dp[0][2]=True.
dp[0][2] = False because the inner dp[1][1] is not yet computed
dp[0][2] is never computed — diagonal cells are skipped
mcqs.explanation: For length=3, i=0, j=2: check s[0]='a'==s[2]='a' (yes) AND dp[1][1]. dp[1][1] = True (base case, single character 'b'). Both conditions true -> dp[0][2] = True. This marks 'aba' as a palindrome. start=0, max_len=3. Return s[0:3]='aba'. Correct.

mcqs.question: Why is the expand-around-center approach O(1) space while DP is O(n^2)?
options: Expand-around-center uses recursion which has O(1) stack depth

Expand-around-center uses only a few pointer variables and the result string — no table. DP explicitly allocates an n x n boolean table to store all dp[i][j] values. For n=1000, that is 10^6 booleans.
DP uses O(n^2) because it considers all pairs twice
They have the same space complexity — O(n^2)
mcqs.explanation: Expand-around-center: variables left, right, result string, c1, c2, candidate — all O(1) or O(output). No table allocated. DP: an explicit n×n 2D list dp = [[False]*n for _ in range(n)]. n=1000 -> 1,000,000 cells. O(n^2). The expand approach achieves the same O(n^2) time without any bookkeeping table.

mcqs.question: s = "abacaba". Which center produces the longest palindrome and what is it?
options: Center at i=1, produces 'aba'
Center at i=3 ('c'), odd-length call: expands to 'abacaba' (full string, length 7)
Center at i=3, even-length call produces length 6
Center at i=0, produces 'a'
mcqs.explanation: s='abacaba', center 'c' at index 3. helper(3,3): s[3]='c'. left=2,right=4: s[2]='a'==s[4]='a'. left=1,right=5: s[1]='b'==s[5]='b'. left=0,right=6: s[0]='a'==s[6]='a'. left=-1,right=7: OOB. Return s[0:7]='abacaba'. Length 7 — the entire string is a palindrome.

mcqs.question: In Manacher's algorithm, what does p[i] represent and how is the original palindrome length extracted?
options: p[i] = index of the palindrome center in the original string

p[i] = radius of the palindrome in the transformed string (with '#' separators). The actual palindrome length in the original string equals p[i] (the '#' characters don't count). If best center is at best_center with radius best_radius: original start = (best_center - best_radius) // 2.
p[i] = number of characters to the left of the palindrome
p[i] = length of the palindrome in the transformed string
mcqs.explanation: In the transformed string '#a#b#a#', center 'b' is at index 3. p[3] = 3 (radius: extends 3 to the left and right including '#' separators). Actual palindrome: 'aba' has length 3 = p[3]. Original start = (3 - 3) // 2 = 0. s[0 : 0+3] = 'aba'. The formula works because every other position in the transformed string is '#', so half the radius corresponds to original characters.

mcqs.question: What would happen if you initialized result = s[0] instead of result = '' in the main loop?
options: An IndexError for empty string s

result = s[0] assumes the answer is at least length 1 (the first character). This is always correct since any single character is a palindrome. The comparison 'if len(candidate) >= len(result)' still works correctly. The problem guarantees len(s) >= 1 so s[0] is safe.
The algorithm returns a palindrome one character shorter
This changes the >= to > behavior
mcqs.explanation: result = s[0] initializes with the first character (length 1). result = '' initializes with an empty string (length 0). Both work because the first iteration (i=0) finds at least c1='s[0]' (length 1), which satisfies len(candidate) >= len(result) in either case. result = s[0] is slightly more robust for the single-character input case; result = '' is also correct since the loop handles it.

mcqs.question: Why is Manacher's algorithm rarely implemented in interviews despite being O(n)?
options: It only works for even-length palindromes

It requires memorizing a non-intuitive algorithm with several tricky boundary cases: the '#' transformation, the mirror formula, the min(right-i, p[mirror]) initialization, and the center/right update condition. Error-free implementation under time pressure is difficult. Expand-around-center achieves the same result with far simpler code and is almost always sufficient for n <= 1000.
It has a higher constant factor than O(n^2) expand-around-center
It is patented and cannot be used commercially
mcqs.explanation: Manacher's has multiple subtleties: the transformation, the mirroring, correctly initializing p[i] from p[mirror] without exceeding the boundary, expanding beyond the boundary, and extracting the original string position from the transformed indices. One mistake in any step gives wrong results. For an interview where n <= 1000 and time is limited, the O(n^2) expand-around-center is the correct practical choice. Mention Manacher's as a follow-up to show awareness.
